title_guess: Northumberland & Yorkshire: Humber Bridge, Bridlington, Alnwick, Bamburgh, Holy Island, Cragside, Kielder, Teesdale

pages.text:
Thursday August 7  070809
Friday 11.15, Simon, Eve & Tonic set off to Northumberland. Tonic's ill.

We stopped to admire the Humber Bridge + walked down to the water's edge through a nature park near an old black windmill. A man arrived in a car & collected sea weed.
      On [the] to Bridlington.
      Pradhma, Godfrey, Emy & Evans welcomed us warmly + picked apples from the tree in their garden + gave us a good supper. It was lovely to see them.
      We drove on + camped at __Muston Grange Caravan Park__, extraordinarily regimented & densely packed caravans. £12.50. Nice people.
      We have had a [?re] series of spills on this trip. I knocked over the dog water bowl. Simon lifted a half-bottle of cider by the stopper & it exploded over the floor. Pradhma tipped water over me at supper. (At lunch today Simon shook the French dressing & it sprayed out of the 20 year old bottle which has developed a split.)
      Worst of all, Tonic had bits of

watery diahorrea all day. He was restless in the night. I heard a watery fart & leapt out of bed to drag open the door & found I was treading in a trail of shine with one foot. It was very dark but I could see it gleaning up towards the door. Simon woke up. Later he said I appeared paralysed. It was truly ghastly. We threshed about with mats & buckets of water & soap.... One of my worst camping nights.
[Friday] Saturday
 After breakfast we attempted damage limitation on Tonic's brown puddles, pouring water on slurry round the camp site.
Then off under glorious sunshine to Whitby. Scarborough looks very fine (no evidence of Butlins) beautiful Edwardian houses. Whitby was peaceful green, green, yellow stone & a blue sea & sky. We

met a nice woman Episcopal Priest from Pennsylvania who remembered about the bird flying through the hall.
We have stopped for lunch on the road towards Teesside.      26 [?Coalhouses]? [illegible] no [illegible],
When we reached the campsite we went for a walk. Tonic had some eggs for supper & felt better. The walk was at first over stiles. Simon decided to take a "short cut" but we couldn't get out of the barbed wire/ditches surrounding the field & ended up walking 4 sides of it. An uneventful night, thank goodness.

Sunday
 We arrived at the Alnick Gardens at 9.30. & got about the only car park slot in the shade, which was just as well for Tonic as the Gardens don't allow dogs. He's still not well. Until 1pm we visited the gardens, having a tour of the Poison Garden & looking at the Grand Cascade, which Simon

captions: boy standing in water sculpture (great anticipation when fountains start)
pages.text:
① boy standing in water sculpture (great anticipation when fountains start)
② girl paddling in ornamental [?gdns]
③ treehouse—
__walk way develops into swing bridges . Restaurants.__
thought was too fussy. The sculptures are fantastic, especially the vortex. Bamboo [illegible] maze . The children drove toy dumper trucks & are encouraged to splash in the fountains.

You need to take changes of clothes. It really is a delight for all ages.
	We had lunch in the van & drove north to Bamburgh. We went to & said evensong at St. Aidan's. We're camping at __Budle Farm Campsite__. The farmer's very nice, He's given us the vet's number for tomorrow, some of his calves' dioralyte for this evening. Tonic doesn't like drinking it. We walked along the beach at high tide & I collected shells. It's very beautiful & looks across to Holy Island.
Simon has brought gin & Tonic on this trip. It makes it even nicer.

__Monday__ boring rain!
	We went to Woolmer for the vet. Tonic's now doped up & recovering. He even walked to __Dunstanborough__, tugging on the lead. After the vet we crossed to Holy Island/Lindisfarne. The castle's shut on Mondays, so we looked at the old Priory. It was orginally set up, economically, to support 10 monks on revenue from surrounding villages. But by the time of the Dissolution there were

captions: Dunstanburgh Castle. Early C4. Lancastrian Stronghold during Wars of the Roses. Built by a nobleman to show off to the king.
pages.text:
only a prior, 2 monks + lots of servants. We drove on to Craster + parked + walked to Dunstanborough + back - glorious sunshine. We've come back to the same lovely little campsite, Budle farm.

[photo of Dunstanburgh Castle from aerial view]

Dunstanburgh Castle. Early C4. Lancastrian Stronghold during Wars of the Roses. Built by a nobleman to show off to the king.

__Tuesday__
   We returned to Lindisfarne to see the Castle, hoping to see the Castle in the morning & then go on to Cragside in the afternoon. However the Castle didn't open until 12. So we packed a lunch + walked around the island, seeing Jekell's beautiful garden. We arrived at the castle at 12 but there was a huge queue, so we decided to return in winter.
   When we reached Cragside, near Rothbury, there was again a huge queue to get into the house. So we saw the Iron Bridge & lakes & Pumphouse & Simon walked to the Powerhouse where Lord Armstrong made hydro electric power. Now we have come on to __Snitter Farm__ __Campsite__, an open view of woods & fields. Tonic is getting better, gradually allowed to eat more but dreadful smells
   Simon & I are sitting in a howling gale + weeping with laughter because his farts are so bad.

__Wednesday__

We had originally intended to go back to Cragside but NT properties are so crowded in August that instead we went west to __Kielder Water__ & walked by the reservoir. It would make a good cycling holiday [drawing] to rent a cottage here & cycle around [drawing] with young children. The path appears to go right round (50m?) + a ferry would take you to 5 jetties around the lake.
Going on southwards, we crossed between Weardale & Teesdale, through Stanhope B6278, glorious empty moorland. We reached Bower & Peter & Elizabeth gave us a lovely supper. We slept in the nearby car park.

__Thursday__

Simon discussed garden projects. At midday we left for a walk & lunch at Arkengarthdale. After lunch in the van, Peter led us on a super walk, up quite a steep hillside where the track disappeared under a slag heap. Lovely wild flowers in meadows by the river & little bridges. We risked the "bull in field sign".
We left for Bham at 4pm + had [~~lunch at~~] supper at Pinnocchio's When we got home.
